name: Dallas Independent School District
districtFact: Dallas ISD boundaries do not match the city of Dallas city limits — some Dallas zip codes fall within Garland ISD, Richardson ISD, or Irving ISD. Always verify with the Dallas ISD school finder at dallasisd.org before signing a lease. Separately, the magnet application cycle for competitive programs like Booker T. Washington HSPVA and STEM Early College opens every January — families who move in February have already missed the current year's window and must wait until the following fall.

about.title: About Dallas Independent School District
about.content:
Dallas Independent School District was founded in 1884 and today serves approximately 145,000 students across more than 230 schools — the second-largest school district in Texas and one of the 20 largest in the United States. The district covers the city of Dallas proper plus portions of six surrounding counties, but its enrollment boundaries do not align with city limits: some addresses within the city of Dallas fall within Garland ISD, Richardson ISD, Carrollton-Farmers Branch ISD, or Irving ISD, while some Dallas ISD schools draw students from addresses just outside the city.

Dallas ISD is one of the most ethnically and linguistically diverse school districts in the country. Approximately 70% of students are Hispanic, 20% are Black, and English learners account for nearly one-third of enrollment. More than 150 languages are spoken across DISD campuses. Over 85% of students qualify for free or reduced-price meals, making Dallas ISD a Title I district with a correspondingly large network of federal support programs.

The school year typically begins in early-to-mid August — earlier than most Texas suburban districts — and ends in late May, meeting the Texas Education Agency's 180-instructional-day requirement. Dallas ISD has historically started a few days earlier than neighboring suburban ISDs like Plano, Frisco, and Allen, which matters for families with children enrolled across multiple DFW districts. The district's size, diversity, and urban geography make it a significantly different experience from the suburban ISDs that surround it.

about.title: Magnet Schools and School Choice in Dallas ISD
about.content:
Dallas ISD operates one of the most extensive magnet and school choice networks in Texas, and nearly all competitive programs run on application timelines that reward early action.

Booker T. Washington High School for the Performing and Visual Arts (HSPVA) is Dallas ISD's flagship magnet and one of the most respected arts high schools in the United States — alumni include Erykah Badu and Norah Jones. HSPVA requires an audition or portfolio submission; applications open in January for the following fall and are reviewed competitively. Students accepted to HSPVA leave their home high school and commute to the HSPVA campus near downtown Dallas.

Skyline High School hosts one of the largest career and technical education (CTE) programs in Texas, with pathways in aviation technology, culinary arts, business, and health science. The STEM Early College High School allows students to begin earning Dallas College credit while still enrolled in DISD — a direct pipeline to an associate degree at no cost for families who plan ahead. Dallas ISD's Dual Language network offers Spanish-English bilingual programs at multiple elementary campuses, with pathways that extend through secondary.

The Dallas ISD School Choice Expo is held each fall — typically October through November — and is the primary opportunity for families to visit programs and ask questions before applications open in January. Families who arrive in Dallas after January have missed the current application cycle and must wait until the following fall. Contact the DISD Choice and Career Education office at dallasisd.org to confirm current program availability and application deadlines before choosing a school-zone address.

about.title: Before You Move to Dallas ISD
about.content:
Four things to confirm before committing to a Dallas address for school enrollment.

Verify you are actually in Dallas ISD — not a neighboring district. Dallas ISD's boundaries do not follow the city of Dallas city limits. Use the Dallas ISD school finder at dallasisd.org with your exact address to confirm district and campus assignment before signing a lease or closing on a home. A Dallas zip code does not guarantee Dallas ISD enrollment.

Map the magnet application calendar against your move date. HSPVA, STEM Early College, Dual Language, and other choice programs open applications every January. A family that moves to Dallas in February has already missed the current cycle — their child would be placed at the neighborhood school and would need to wait until next January to apply to a magnet. If access to HSPVA or another competitive program is a priority, plan to be enrolled in DISD before the January window.

Know the Texas September 1 kindergarten cutoff. Texas requires children to be 5 years old by September 1 to enroll in kindergarten — earlier than many other states. Families relocating from Virginia (September 30 cutoff), Connecticut (January 1), or other states with later cutoffs may find that a child who was eligible in their previous state does not yet qualify in Texas. Children who miss the cutoff must wait a full year.

Plan for August heat and back-to-school logistics. Dallas ISD starts in early-to-mid August, when Dallas temperatures regularly exceed 100°F. Outdoor activities, recess, and athletics are subject to UIL and district heat protocols. Uniform and supply shopping is best done by late July — popular sizes at stores near DISD schools deplete before the first week of school.

content: Suburban DFW districts (Plano, Frisco, Garland) have their own Spring Break dates that may differ from Dallas ISD's by one to two days. If coordinating schedules across districts, verify each one separately.
Dallas ISD magnet and choice school enrollment opens in January. Attend a fall choice expo and apply early — School of Science and Engineering and other top programs have limited seats.
Dallas summer temperatures regularly exceed 105°F. Back-to-school shopping is best done by late July — popular uniform and supply stores near Dallas ISD schools deplete common sizes before August 8.

Dallas ISD boundaries do not follow city limits. Verify your assigned district and school using the official Dallas ISD school finder — some addresses within the city of Dallas fall within neighboring districts like Garland ISD or Irving ISD.

districtFaqs.q: Is Dallas ISD the same as UT Dallas (UTD) or University of Dallas (UD)?
districtFaqs.a: No. Dallas Independent School District (Dallas ISD) is the K-12 public school system serving the city of Dallas. UT Dallas (the University of Texas at Dallas) and the University of Dallas (a private Catholic university in Irving) are separate institutions with their own academic calendars. Neither university's semester schedule has any connection to Dallas ISD school dates.

districtFaqs.q: When does Dallas ISD magnet school enrollment open?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD's school choice and magnet enrollment process typically opens in January for the following school year. The district holds choice expos in the fall (October–November) where families can learn about available programs. Applications for competitive programs like the School of Science and Engineering and bilingual academies should be submitted as early as possible — seats fill quickly and late applicants are placed on waitlists.

districtFaqs.q: Does Dallas ISD serve all addresses within the city of Dallas?
districtFaqs.a: No. Dallas ISD boundaries do not match the city of Dallas city limits. Some addresses within the city of Dallas fall within neighboring independent school districts, including Garland ISD, Richardson ISD, Irving ISD, Carrollton-Farmers Branch ISD, and others. Conversely, some Dallas ISD schools serve students from addresses just outside the city. Families should verify their assigned school district using the official Dallas ISD school finder at dallasisd.org — do not assume that a Dallas mailing address means Dallas ISD enrollment.

districtFaqs.q: How does Dallas ISD compare with Plano ISD or Frisco ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD, Plano ISD, and Frisco ISD are separate, independent school districts in the DFW metroplex with their own academic calendars, boundaries, and school choice programs. Plano ISD and Frisco ISD are suburban districts serving northern DFW communities and are entirely separate from Dallas ISD. Spring Break dates in these districts typically fall within a day or two of Dallas ISD's, but exact dates are not guaranteed to align — always verify each district's official calendar. Families buying or renting in north Dallas suburbs should check whether their specific address falls within Dallas ISD or a suburban district before finalizing enrollment.

districtFaqs.q: Does Dallas ISD follow the same calendar as Fort Worth ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD and Fort Worth ISD are separate school districts that each set their own academic calendars. The two districts typically start within a few days of each other and often share the same Spring Break week, but individual non-school days, teacher workdays, and early release days can differ. Families coordinating between the two districts — or with relatives on opposite sides of DFW — should check each district's official calendar rather than assuming they match. Families in mid-cities communities like Grand Prairie or Irving should note that those cities have their own separate ISDs entirely distinct from both Dallas and Fort Worth.

districtFaqs.q: What are Dallas ISD school hours?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD school start and dismissal times vary by campus and grade level — they are not uniform across the district. High schools generally start earliest (around 7:00–7:30 AM, dismissing around 2:30–3:00 PM), while elementary schools typically start later (around 7:45–8:15 AM). Middle schools fall in between. The staggered schedule coordinates with Dallas ISD's transportation routing, which runs buses across all three levels in sequence. Specific hours for any campus are listed on that school's page at dallasisd.org. Families with children at different school levels should check each school's bell schedule separately before arranging carpool or childcare.

districtFaqs.q: Does Dallas ISD provide bus transportation?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD provides bus transportation for eligible students based on distance from their assigned school. Students who live beyond the walk-zone threshold are generally eligible for bus service; those living within walking distance are expected to walk or receive a car drop-off. Families new to Dallas ISD should confirm bus eligibility and stop locations through their assigned school after completing enrollment — bus routes are typically finalized in late July before the August school start. Families relocating mid-year should ask about temporary transportation arrangements when completing enrollment paperwork at the district.

districtFaqs.q: How does Dallas ISD handle bad weather and winter ice closures?
districtFaqs.a: North Texas experiences ice storms more frequently than snow, and Dallas ISD's road network — including elevated highways and bridges — can become dangerously icy even with light precipitation. Dallas ISD builds inclement weather days into its calendar each year. When closures exceed those built-in days, the district may convert teacher workdays to instructional days or extend the school year into June. Closure and delay decisions are typically announced by 5 AM on the affected day via the Dallas ISD website, the district's notification system, and local news. Families should register for Dallas ISD alerts at dallasisd.org before November — ice events can arrive early in winter without significant warning.

districtFaqs.q: What are the grading periods in Dallas ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD divides its school year into six grading periods, each approximately six weeks long — a standard structure for Texas school districts. Report cards are issued at the end of each grading period. Progress reports are typically distributed at the midpoint of each period, giving families advance notice before report cards. The six-period structure means grades are evaluated more frequently than in semester-based systems: a student who struggles early in a grading period has approximately six weeks to improve before the grade is recorded. The exact grading period dates for each school year are listed in the official DISD calendar at dallasisd.org.

districtFaqs.q: When does kindergarten registration open in Dallas ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD kindergarten registration typically opens in February or March for the following fall school year. Texas requires children to be 5 years old by September 1 to enroll in kindergarten. Required documents include: certified birth certificate, current immunization records meeting Texas requirements, and proof of Dallas ISD residency (lease, utility bill, or mortgage statement). Families relocating to Dallas ISD in spring or summer should contact their assigned elementary school directly rather than waiting for a district-wide registration event — schools can often begin the enrollment process before the formal district registration period opens. Pre-kindergarten (PreK) programs are available for eligible 4-year-olds; eligibility criteria and enrollment dates differ from kindergarten.

districtFaqs.q: How do I look up my child's school in Dallas ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Use the Dallas ISD school finder at dallasisd.org with your exact home address to identify your assigned elementary, middle, and high school. Do not assume school assignment based on your zip code or neighborhood — Dallas ISD boundaries run through residential areas and do not align with the city of Dallas city limits. Some addresses within Dallas city limits are assigned to neighboring districts such as Garland ISD, Richardson ISD, or Irving ISD. If your address returns no result or an unexpected school, contact Dallas ISD enrollment directly at dallasisd.org to confirm. School assignments can also change when Dallas ISD redraws attendance boundaries, so verify your assignment each year rather than relying on a previous year's result.

districtFaqs.q: What are Dallas ISD early release days?
districtFaqs.a: Dallas ISD schedules early release days throughout the school year — students are dismissed 1–2 hours early on these days to allow for teacher professional development and collaborative planning. These days appear on the official Dallas ISD academic calendar at dallasisd.org and are not holidays; students attend school for part of the day. The specific dates and number of early release days vary by school year. Childcare providers and afterschool programs may or may not cover early release dismissal times — confirm your provider's policy before the school year starts. Early release days are among the most overlooked calendar details for families new to Dallas ISD.

livingHere.intro: Dallas is one of the fastest-growing metros in the US, home to more Fortune 500 headquarters than any other American city except New York, and the anchor of the sprawling DFW metroplex. For families relocating to the area, understanding the city's geography, climate, and corporate culture helps frame how the school calendar fits into daily life.

highlights.label: DFW International Airport
highlights.detail: One of the world's largest airports by area, with nonstop service to over 200 destinations. Spring Break departures book out weeks in advance — Dallas ISD's mid-March break aligns with peak DFW travel demand, particularly to beach destinations and Mexico.

highlights.label: Corporate Headquarters and Mid-Year Enrollment
highlights.detail: American Airlines, AT&T, Toyota North America, and dozens of other major companies are headquartered in the DFW area. Corporate relocation cycles drive consistent mid-year enrollment into Dallas ISD — transfers from California, New York, and Illinois are common in fall and January as companies move employees into DFW. Families arriving via corporate transfer should contact Dallas ISD enrollment in advance; the district can often complete school placement within a few days of a completed application.

highlights.label: Tornado Season
highlights.detail: North Texas sits in Tornado Alley. Peak tornado risk runs March–May, overlapping with STAAR testing season and the weeks after Spring Break. Dallas ISD uses shelter-in-place protocols and may delay dismissal during severe weather watches.

highlights.label: Extreme Summer Heat
highlights.detail: Dallas regularly records temperatures above 105°F in July and August. UIL heat protocols restrict outdoor practices during early back-to-school weeks. Families should expect limited outdoor athletic activity the first two weeks of the school year.

highlights.label: State Fair of Texas — Fall Field Trips and Traffic
highlights.detail: The State Fair of Texas, held annually at Fair Park in Dallas from late September through mid-October, is one of the country's largest state fairs and draws over two million visitors. Dallas ISD schools use the Fair as a field trip destination — particularly for science and agriculture curriculum — and students commonly attend on school days during the run. The Fair's 24-day schedule falls entirely within Dallas ISD's first quarter, meaning traffic on I-30 and around Fair Park is significantly heavier on school mornings and afternoons. Families driving through east Dallas to school drop-off during the Fair should budget extra commute time.

highlights.label: Professional Sports and School Life
highlights.detail: Dallas ISD schools are deeply embedded in DFW sports culture. UIL varsity football games on Friday nights are a major community event from late August through November — game-night traffic near high school campuses and stadium areas is significant. Rangers postseason games in October occasionally run past 11 PM on school nights, worth factoring into homework and sleep routines during the first quarter. Graduation ceremonies for Dallas ISD high schools are commonly held at American Airlines Center and Toyota Music Factory — families should check venue parking and event schedules in May, as multiple graduations may be scheduled on the same weekend.

highlights.label: Dallas ISD Signature Programs
highlights.detail: Dallas ISD operates several nationally recognized magnet programs that are distinct from any other DFW district. Booker T. Washington High School for the Performing and Visual Arts (HSPVA) is one of the country's most respected arts magnet high schools and draws competitive applicants from across the district. Skyline High School hosts one of Texas's largest career and technical education programs, with pathways in aviation, culinary arts, and business. The STEM Early College High School allows students to earn college credit through Dallas College while enrolled in DISD. Dallas Promise provides tuition-free access to Dallas College for Dallas ISD graduates — families with high schoolers should track GPA and attendance requirements during DISD years to maintain eligibility. Families interested in any of these programs should attend a Dallas ISD Choice Expo in fall (typically October–November) — applications open in January and competitive programs fill early.

highlights.label: Dallas ISD Annual Planning Rhythm
highlights.detail: Dallas ISD follows a predictable annual cycle that families benefit from mapping at the start of each school year. Early-to-mid August: school begins alongside UIL athletic season and Friday Night Football. September–October: State Fair of Texas runs through Fair Park, affecting east Dallas commute routes; Choice Expos are held for families researching next year's magnet and specialty programs. January: magnet and choice applications open — the deadline for Booker T. Washington HSPVA, STEM Early College, and other competitive programs. March–April: STAAR state testing season begins in the weeks after Spring Break, with tornado risk peaking simultaneously. May: graduation season — ceremonies at American Airlines Center and Toyota Music Factory run on consecutive weekends, with parking and downtown traffic affected. Mapping these milestones at the start of August makes the school year significantly more manageable.

compareIntro: Dallas ISD and its DFW neighbors each set their own academic calendars independently. Spring Break in Dallas ISD typically falls in mid-to-late March, but Plano ISD, Frisco ISD, and Fort Worth ISD may start a few days earlier or later — never assume alignment. The bigger differences are usually the first and last day: Dallas ISD sometimes starts earlier than suburban DFW peers and often ends in late May while some suburbs run into early June. Families managing children across multiple DFW districts, or coordinating childcare with a suburban provider, should pull and compare each district's full calendar rather than relying on a general 'DFW school schedule.'

relatedDistricts.name: Houston Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Both are large, majority-minority urban Texas districts with diverse student populations and extensive magnet programs, but they sit 240 miles apart and operate entirely separate calendars. Houston ISD faces Atlantic hurricane risk that Dallas ISD does not; Dallas ISD faces tornado and ice storm risk that rarely affects Houston. Spring Break weeks sometimes align (both tend to cluster in mid-March), sometimes don't — verify each year. Houston ISD has been operating under TEA state conservatorship, a governance structure Dallas ISD does not currently have, which affects how Houston ISD's calendar decisions are made.

relatedDistricts.name: Fort Worth Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Fort Worth ISD is Dallas ISD's closest geographic peer — both serve large, diverse, majority-minority urban populations with extensive magnet and CTE programs. The two districts typically start within a few days of each other and share the same Spring Break week in most years, but individual non-school days, teacher workdays, and early release days regularly differ. Families in mid-cities communities between Dallas and Fort Worth (Grand Prairie, Irving, Arlington) should note that those cities have their own separate ISDs that are not part of either Dallas ISD or Fort Worth ISD.

relatedDistricts.name: Austin Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Austin ISD serves a smaller, differently demographic urban population 195 miles southwest of Dallas. Both are Texas urban districts that must meet the same 180-day TEA requirement, so calendar structure is similar, but Austin ISD's Spring Break timing can differ by a week or more from Dallas ISD's. Austin ISD enrolls roughly 70,000 students — about half Dallas ISD's size — and operates a different portfolio of magnet and specialty programs. Families comparing the two cities for relocation should check both districts' full calendars each year rather than assuming statewide Texas calendar uniformity.
